dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a Sick Person at Manor Care Nursing Home in [CITY].
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What's going on with the patient?
We responded to Manor Care Nursing Home, room 128A, for a 59-year-old male with altered mental status.
He normally talks well, but tonight he was quiet and not acting like himself.
Got it. He's alert, but non-verbal and disoriented? Any signs of pain or new physical changes?
Can you tell me where you are? What's today's date?
He's unresponsive to verbal prompts but gives incomprehensible sounds at times.
Eyes are open but not following commands. No visual tracking observed.
Verbal responses are incoherent, but he does understand some commands-like nodding no when asked if he's in pain.
Right arm drift noted, but we're told that is baseline. Otherwise no major motor function loss.
Semi-Fowler's position to help maintain airway patency.
No signs of airway obstruction or gurgling, and breathing is audible but not labored.
Respirations are 29-tachypneic, but depth appears average.
SpO2 is at 100% on initial assessment.
No signs of increased respiratory effort, flaring nostrils, or accessory muscle use.
Pulse is 94, strong, and regular with good peripheral perfusion.
Blood pressure at 122/84 initially; trending upward to 155/90 shortly afterward.
Stable circulation without signs of deterioration.
Who first noticed these symptoms and when did they start?
The night nurse noticed them after shift change-less than an hour ago.
Any idea when he last ate or drank?
Not exactly, but it was during the night shift.
What about known medical conditions or medications?
He has a history of hypertension and ulcerative colitis. Also, he's allergic to penicillin G.
He indicated no pain and no discomfort when asked directly.
There is definite left-sided facial droop as observed by staff.
No described extraocular movement problems or eye size asymmetry.
Right arm drift present. Otherwise, motor and sensory functions are unclear given non-verbal status.
Skin turgor and appearance are normal. No rashes or injuries observed.
Stable breath sounds, no wheezes or crackles.
Sinus rhythm noted on EKG, but there are ST wave and T wave abnormalities.
No abdominal distension, tenderness, or guarding observed.
Abdomen is flat and soft. No visible abnormalities.
A UTI-type odor is present in the room.
No signs of pulmonary distress like asymmetrical breathing or raised work of breathing.
SpO2 remains steady at 97-100%.
Blood sugar is 221 mg/dL-hyperglycemic.
Blood pressure is 155/90-elevated but stable from prior.
Heart rate decreased from 94 to 88 over time-still within reasonable range.
Intravenous access established via 20G LAC in the arm.
IV initiated successfully at 22:51, registered in the report.
Stroke/TIA protocol will be used due to facial droop and altered mental status.
UTI-related symptoms may contribute, but neurological concern remains primary.
Re-checked SpO2-97%.
Glucose level not available at this time-possible monitor issue.
Heart rate remains at 88 with no arrhythmias noted.
Respiratory rate is still 29, elevated but stable.
Blood pressure of 155/90 continues with no warning signs.
Still no pain or discomfort indicated-motor and verbal responses unchanged.
12-lead EKG done shows LVH and lateral ischemia.
We will transport to [COMPANY_NAME] under Stroke/TIA protocol.
Patient is secured onto the stretcher and ready for ambulance.
Multiple stroke indicators, including facial droop and EKG anomalies, concern for underlying conditions like possible pneumonia.
Altered mental status noted, and his response to pain is at baseline-pain is at 0.
Transporting in semi-Fowler's with briefings to the ER regarding neurological concerns and possible UTI.
ER is alerted for possible stroke and EKG findings.
No new complaints or complications-AMS and altered performance remain confirmed.
Patient transitioned to ambulance without incident-secured for transport.
Patient is alert per his altered baseline-with minimal signs of active distress.
Stroke alert system has been activated. En route coordination is open with ER staff.
Initial 12-lead EKG performed per protocol-moderate left ventricular hypertrophy noted.
All remaining procedures are documented in the field report. We're waiting for arrival.
Missed intake chances can skew signs of hypoglycemia or hydration-seen as non-contributing at this time.
Patient feels cool to the touch-no fever or flush observed, and glucose measured at 0 at one point-troubleshooting sensors.
No recent changes in medications or acute allergic reactions noted.
Chest movement is symmetrical and rises with expiratory effort.
No acute changes observed since last assessment-patient remains non-verbal but stable.
Blood pressure now trending from 122/84 to 155/90-signs of early hypertension.
No new signs of neurological worsening or stroke-like progression during transport.
Hospital entry at [COMPANY_NAME] is expected within 10 minutes-ambulance on route clear.
Patient-handover book towards ER can be signed now-summary compiled and complete.
Clock noted at 23:22-time for next set of vitals either on board or upon ED entry.
Arrival here complete-good hand-over with onsite nurse ready.
This patient has elevated risk markers-please continue with urgent imaging or neurological triage-given LVH findings.
Patient is non-verbal-showed baseline signs and no pain high (pain at 0).
Data out of protocol-same suspected reasons for Stroke/TIA and UTI-type infection.
No signs of early deterioration-no patients noting rapid changes of status or adverse function.
Altered mental status and neurological concerns prompted alert and care pathway-consistent with transported grips.
Respiration and heart rate monitored stable throughout the transfer.
IV access confirmed intact throughout transport-no recorded spill-outs or lock-up issues.
Reevaluated for breathing status: still 29 per. No new onset of labored effort or hypoxia.
Post transport EKG needed once on board for definitive evaluation.
SpO2 remains normal-no need for added oxygen supplementation thus far.
Final tally for ER: 59-year-old male non-verbal resident with possible stroke symptoms and possible UTI or pneumonia marker.
Preparing final medical report and transport summary for handover.
No changes in awake level, breathing, pain level for last 15 minutes.
Blood pressure today trending from 122/84 to 155/90-signs of early hypertension.
Final vital signs, medications, and EKG findings are confirmed. All reports finalized.
Clock noted at 23:22-time for next set of vitals either on board or upon ED entry.
Patient transferred successfully to hospital staff-handover complete.
Final handover notes acknowledged by ER staff-Stroke/TIA and possible UTI confirmed per care documentation.
Stroke/TIA protocol completed-patient arrived at emergency department with No acute functional changes or new stroke symptoms during transport.
